era_context: The War of Spanish Succession (1701–1714) was in its third year, with major military campaigns in Europe and North America. The Qing Dynasty under Kangxi was at its height; the Ottoman Empire remained a significant regional power; and European colonial powers were consolidating control in the Americas and Asia. The Great Northern War (1700–1721) between Russia and Sweden was ongoing.

events.title: Founding of St. Petersburg
events.description: On 16 May 1703 (27 May in the Julian calendar used in Russia), Peter I of Russia founded the fortress of St. Peter and Paul on the Neva River delta, marking the beginning of the city of St. Petersburg. The fortress was constructed as part of Peter's campaign to secure control of the Baltic coast during the Great Northern War against Sweden. St. Petersburg would become Peter's new capital, replacing Moscow, and would develop into a major European city. The founding represented Peter's determination to modernize Russia and establish it as a European power.
key_figures: Peter I (Peter the Great), Tsar of Russia

sources.name: Russian imperial records and Peter's correspondence
Historiographic consensus on Peter the Great and Russian history

events.certainty_note: The founding of St. Petersburg is precisely documented in Russian imperial records. The date is well-established, though the Julian/Gregorian calendar difference must be noted.

events.title: Battle of Hochstädt
events.description: On 20 September 1703, French forces under Marshal Villars defeated an Imperial army under General Styrum near Hochstädt in Bavaria. The French victory temporarily secured French control of Bavaria and demonstrated French military capability in the region. However, the victory did not decisively resolve the Bavarian campaign, and the strategic situation remained contested.

sources.name: Military histories of the War of Spanish Succession

events.certainty_note: Well-documented military engagement with contemporary accounts from multiple sources. Date and outcome are uncontested.

events.title: Kangxi Emperor Receives Papal Legate
events.description: In 1703, the Kangxi Emperor received a papal legate (representative of the Pope) at the imperial court. This diplomatic exchange reflected the complex relationship between the Qing court and the Catholic Church, particularly regarding the Chinese Rites Controversy. The Kangxi Emperor was interested in Western scientific knowledge brought by Jesuit missionaries but was also concerned about the religious and political implications of Christian missionary activity.

sources.name: Qing imperial records and Jesuit correspondence

events.certainty_note: Diplomatic exchanges between the Kangxi court and the Catholic Church are well-documented; however, specific events in 1703 are less precisely dated than broader patterns of engagement.

events.title: Queen Anne's War: Raid on Wells, Maine
events.description: In August 1703, French and Native American forces raided the settlement of Wells, Maine, killing several colonists and taking others captive. The raid was part of Queen Anne's War (1702–1713) and demonstrated the ongoing conflict between European colonial powers and their Native American allies on the frontier.
key_figures: Native American and French raiders (names not consistently recorded)
events.certainty_note: The raid is attested in colonial records, though specific details vary between sources. The general occurrence and approximate date are well-established.

events.title: Great Storm of 1703
events.description: On 26 November 1703, a severe storm struck England and Western Europe, causing widespread damage and loss of life. The storm, which lasted several days, was one of the most destructive weather events recorded in early modern Europe. Estimates suggest that 8,000 people died in England alone, with significant damage to buildings, forests, and shipping. The storm was extensively documented in contemporary accounts and had economic and social impacts across the region.

sources.name: Contemporary accounts and diaries (e.g., John Evelyn, Samuel Pepys's diary)
Historiographic consensus on early modern European weather and climate

events.certainty_note: The Great Storm of 1703 is extensively documented in contemporary accounts and later historical records. The date and general impact are well-established.

disconfirming_evidence: The founding of St. Petersburg is sometimes presented as an immediate triumph; however, the city's early years were marked by difficult conditions, high mortality among workers, and slow initial development. The strategic value of the location was not immediately apparent, and the decision to make it the capital was controversial. The Great Storm of 1703 is sometimes attributed to specific meteorological causes; however, modern analysis suggests it was likely a combination of factors, and the exact meteorological mechanisms remain debated.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1703 is rich in European military and political history, particularly regarding the War of Spanish Succession. Russian history is well-documented through imperial records, particularly regarding Peter the Great's reign. Colonial North American history is documented through colonial records. Qing China is documented through imperial records. However, the historical record is heavily weighted toward elite political and military events; indigenous perspectives in North America are largely filtered through colonial sources. African and Southeast Asian history is underrepresented in surviving documentation.

graph_edges.note: St. Petersburg's founding led to its establishment as the Russian capital in 1712
The Bavarian campaign continued with the Battle of Blenheim in 1704
Both are raids during Queen Anne's War in colonial North America
